packages.name: awk
packages.source: Brian W. Kernighan's One True Awk (github.com/onetrueawk/awk) 2024.12.25
packages.porting_notes: One True Awk by Brian Kernighan ported to AmigaOS 3.x. Key changes: popen/pclose replaced with amiport_emu_popen (temp-file emulation); system() uses amiport_emu_system returning AmigaDOS RC; MB_CUR_MAX forced to 1 (no wchar on AmigaOS); random()/srandom() mapped to rand()/srand(); /dev/null replaced with NIL:; stat()/S_ISDIR() use amiport_stat(); fcntl(FD_CLOEXEC) stubbed as no-op; %zu format specifiers replaced with %lu + cast; compiled with -std=gnu99 (ADR-022). Bug fix: upstream uses %.30g for integer formatting -- at 30 significant digits IEEE 754 double precision noise becomes visible in libnix output. Fixed to %.15g (max meaningful precision for 64-bit double). All 134 FS-UAE tests pass.
packages.known_limitations: Pipe commands (print | "cmd", "cmd" | getline) use temp files -- not concurrent. Shell syntax in pipe commands limited to AmigaDOS (no &&, ||, backticks). Multibyte/UTF-8 disabled -- operates on raw bytes (correct for ISO 8859-1). ENVIRON array is empty (AmigaOS has no environ). SIGFPE not delivered -- math errors caught via errno. Exit code for syntax errors is 2 (below AmigaDOS RETURN_WARN threshold of 5).
packages.readme:
Short:        Pattern scanning and processing language
Type:         dev/lang
Architecture: m68k-amigaos >= 3.0
Uploader:     duncan (at) bowring (dot) us
Author:       Brian W. Kernighan (ported by Duncan Bowring)
Version:      2024.12.25

awk - Pattern scanning and processing language

The "One True AWK" by Brian Kernighan -- the canonical implementation
by the original author. Replaces ATT-awk-1_0 (1994) with 30 years of
bug fixes and improvements.

Ported by Duncan Bowring using amiport (AI-assisted porting toolkit).
Project: https://github.com/bdgscotland/amiport

Cross-compiled with m68k-amigaos-gcc for AmigaOS 3.x (68000+).
Tested in vamos emulator and FS-UAE.

Usage:
  awk 'pattern { action }' file ...
  awk -f progfile file ...
  awk -F: '{ print $1 }' /etc/passwd

Examples:
  awk '{ print NR, $0 }' file.txt       Number each line
  awk -F, '{ print $2 }' data.csv       Print second CSV field
  awk '/pattern/' file.txt               Print matching lines (like grep)
  awk '{ sum += $1 } END { print sum }' numbers.txt   Sum column 1
  awk 'NF > 0' file.txt                 Remove blank lines
  awk '{ print length, $0 }' file.txt   Print line lengths

Limitations on AmigaOS:
  - Pipe commands (print | "cmd") use temp files, not concurrent pipes
  - Shell syntax in pipe commands limited to AmigaDOS (no &&, ||)
  - Multibyte/UTF-8 disabled -- operates on raw bytes (ISO 8859-1)
  - ENVIRON array is empty (AmigaOS has no environ)

Includes full source code (original and ported versions)
and PORT.md documenting every transformation applied.

packages.name: basename
packages.description: Strip directory and suffix from filenames
packages.porting_notes: Minimal port -- 76-line pure C89 program with no Tier 2/3 issues. Transformations: err.h shim, pledge() stubbed, __dead attribute removed, __progname defined directly (weak symbol fix), exit codes changed to AmigaOS values (1->10), amiport_expand_argv() for wildcard expansion, atexit() cleanup for argv. No struct returns, no alignment concerns, no 64-bit types. Note: only handles / as path separator; AmigaOS : volume separator is treated as part of the filename (POSIX behavior). 32/32 FS-UAE tests pass.
packages.known_limitations: Only handles / as path separator. AmigaOS paths with : (e.g. Work:file.txt) are treated as a single filename component. This matches POSIX behavior but differs from what an Amiga-native implementation might do.

packages.readme:
Short:        GNU arbitrary precision calculator
Type:         util/cli
Architecture: m68k-amigaos >= 3.0
Uploader:     duncan (at) bowring (dot) us
Author:       Philip A. Nelson (ported by Duncan Bowring)
Version:      1.07.1

bc - GNU arbitrary precision calculator

Original author: Philip A. Nelson
Ported by Duncan Bowring using amiport (AI-assisted porting toolkit).
Project: https://github.com/bdgscotland/amiport

Cross-compiled with m68k-amigaos-gcc for AmigaOS 3.x (68000+).
Tested on FS-UAE with Workbench 3.1 (37/37 tests pass).

bc is an arbitrary precision calculator language supporting variables,
functions, and a C-like syntax. The -l flag loads a math library with
transcendental functions (sin, cos, arctan, ln, exp, bessel).

Usage:
  bc [-hlqswv] [file ...]

  bc                   Interactive calculator
  bc -l                Load math library (e, l, s, c, a, j functions)
  bc -q script.bc      Run script without welcome banner
  bc -c script.bc      Compile to bytecode (don't execute)

Examples:
  echo "2^10" | bc               Result: 1024
  echo "scale=10; 4*a(1)" | bc -l   Pi to 10 places: 3.1415926532

Replaces the ixemul-dependent bc 1.6 on Aminet (dev/gg/bc-1.6)
with a standalone noixemul binary. No runtime library dependencies.

Includes full source code (original POSIX and ported Amiga versions)
and PORT.md documenting every transformation applied.
packages.porting_notes: GNU bc 1.07.1 is a yacc/lex-generated multi-file port (12 .c files). Key transformations: signal(SIGINT) replaced with amiport_check_break() polling in the execute loop for Ctrl-C support; isatty() replaced with amiport_isatty(); getenv() replaced with amiport_getenv() with tracked cleanup via atexit(); fread()-based YY_INPUT replaces read()/fileno() in the scanner; all bc_exit(1) calls changed to bc_exit(10) for Amiga RETURN_ERROR (8 instances across main.c, scan.c, load.c, util.c); exit(YY_EXIT_FAILURE) changed to bc_exit(20) for RETURN_FAIL; fclose(yyin) guarded against stdin close. Bundled GNU getopt used instead of broken libnix getopt_long (crash-patterns #17). --allow-multiple-definition linker flag needed for bundled getopt vs libnix symbol clash. libmath.h generated via bootstrap. 37/37 FS-UAE tests pass including real-world stress tests: factorial(50), fibonacci(100), 10K loop iterations, pi to 100 digits. Perf-optimizer identified DIVU→shift opportunities and free-list pooling for number.c hot loops (noted, not yet applied).
packages.known_limitations: No readline/libedit line editing in interactive mode. BC_ENV_ARGS only works if set in AmigaOS ENV: variable. random() mapped to rand() — adequate for bc's non-cryptographic use.

Original author: The Regents of the University of California

Usage:
  cksum [-o 1|2|3] [file ...]

  Computes and displays CRC checksum and byte count for each file.

and PORT.md documenting every transformation applied.
packages.porting_notes: Multi-file port (cksum.c, crc.c, crc32.c, sum.c) from FreeBSD. Higher stack (64KB) needed for CRC table initialization. 37 tests cover all three algorithm modes (-o 1/2/3), binary files, empty files, multi-file checksumming, and stdin mode.

comm - Compare two sorted files line by line

Original author: University of California, Berkeley (Case Larsen)
Ported by Duncan Bowring using amiport (AI-assisted porting toolkit).
Project: https://github.com/bdgscotland/amiport

Cross-compiled with m68k-amigaos-gcc for AmigaOS 3.x (68000+).
Tested in vamos emulator and FS-UAE.

Usage:
  comm [-123f] file1 file2

  comm file1 file2       Show all three columns
  comm -12 file1 file2   Show only common lines (column 3)
  comm -23 file1 file2   Show lines only in file1
  comm -13 file1 file2   Show lines only in file2
  comm -f file1 file2    Case-insensitive comparison

Includes full source code (original POSIX and ported Amiga versions)
and PORT.md documenting every transformation applied.
packages.porting_notes: Single-file EASY port (179 lines, pure C89). Standard Tier 1 transformations: pledge() stubbed as macro, exit(1) changed to exit(10) (RETURN_ERROR), LINE_MAX guarded with #ifndef fallback, __stack = 32768 cookie added, $VER string added. Review-amiga improvements: amiport_check_break() added to both comparison loops batched every 64 iterations (Ctrl-C handling); fclose(stdout) replaced with fflush(stdout) to avoid closing shell handle; line buffers made static to reduce stack pressure. Perf-optimizer: printf replaced with fputs (avoids format dispatch overhead on 7MHz 68000); strcoll replaced with strcmp (libnix has no real locale support). Memory audit CLEAN -- no dynamic allocation, no leaks, all exit paths safe. 30/30 FS-UAE tests pass including flag combinations, error paths, edge cases (empty files, single-line files, no-common-lines), long lines, stress test with 200-line files, and real-world use cases.
packages.known_limitations: strcoll() collation depends on AmigaOS locale; for non-ASCII filenames results may differ from POSIX sort order. -f flag uses tolower() which only handles ASCII case folding (no locale-aware case comparison on AmigaOS 3.x).

packages.name: diff
packages.porting_notes: The Hunt-McIlroy core algorithm was pure C and fully portable, but the port required two new shim functions (fnmatch and scandir), a 256KB stack cookie due to hidden AmigaDOS stack depth, and a case-insensitive filename comparison fix — AmigaOS filesystems never distinguish file case but strcmp() was used throughout diffdir.c. A subtle argv pointer-arithmetic bug in set_argstr() also caused a 4GB allocation on vamos before being fixed.
packages.known_limitations: -I pattern (ignore matching lines) is disabled as it requires a bundled regex library. Long option names (--unified, --recursive) are not supported; use short options. File timestamps in unified/context headers depend on Locale timezone settings.

false - Return false value (OpenBSD)

Original author: The Regents of the University of California
Ported by Duncan Bowring using amiport (AI-assisted porting toolkit).
Project: https://github.com/bdgscotland/amiport

Cross-compiled with m68k-amigaos-gcc for AmigaOS 3.x (68000+).
Tested in FS-UAE with AmigaOS 3.1.

Usage:
  false

  Returns exit code 10 (RETURN_ERROR). Useful in AmigaDOS scripts to
  force an error condition or test error-handling paths.

Note: On AmigaOS, exit code 10 is used instead of Unix exit(1) because
AmigaDOS scripts test with IF ERROR (>=10), not IF WARN (>=5).

Examples:
  false                    ; returns RETURN_ERROR (10) always
  false ; IF ERROR FAIL    ; always triggers the FAIL branch

Includes full source code (original POSIX and ported Amiga versions)
and PORT.md documenting every transformation applied.
packages.porting_notes: Key Amiga-specific change: exit code changed from 1 to 10 (RETURN_ERROR). On AmigaOS, exit(1) falls below the IF ERROR threshold (10) and scripts cannot detect it. All 14 tests verify the binary always returns exactly 10 regardless of arguments.

packages.readme:
Short:        OpenBSD grep (pattern search) for AmigaOS
Type:         util/cli

Uploader:     duncan (at) bowring (dot) us

grep — OpenBSD grep (pattern search) for AmigaOS

Original author: James Howard / Dag-Erling Smorgrav
Ported by Duncan Bowring using amiport (AI-assisted porting toolkit).
Project: https://github.com/bdgscotland/amiport

Cross-compiled with m68k-amigaos-gcc for AmigaOS 3.x (68000+).
Tested in vamos emulator and FS-UAE (Amiga 1200).

Usage:
  grep [options] pattern [file ...]

  grep hello file.txt          Search for "hello" in file.txt
  grep -i HELLO file.txt       Case-insensitive search
  grep -n pattern file.txt     Show line numbers
  grep -v pattern file.txt     Show non-matching lines
  grep -c pattern file.txt     Count matching lines
  grep -E "foo|bar" file.txt   Extended regex (alternation)
  grep -F "a.b" file.txt       Fixed string (no regex)
  grep -R pattern dir          Recursive directory search
  grep -B2 -A2 pattern file    Show 2 lines before/after match

Exit codes (Amiga-native):
  0  = match found (RETURN_OK)
  5  = no match found (RETURN_WARN)
  10 = error (RETURN_ERROR)

Note: -Z (decompress) is not supported (no zlib on AmigaOS).

Includes full source code (original POSIX and ported Amiga versions)
and PORT.md documenting every transformation applied.
packages.porting_notes: Multi-file port requiring removal of mmap and zlib dependencies, replacement of fts(3) directory traversal with an opendir/readdir walker, and manual emulation of REG_STARTEND for the Tier 2 regex engine. Two critical bugs were found and fixed post-publication: exit codes were always 0 due to missing braces, and recursive search (-R) silently skipped subdirectories because AmigaOS filesystems never populate d_type.
packages.known_limitations: No gzip/zlib support (-Z flag disabled). Recursive search limited to 32 directory levels. POSIX character classes ([:alpha:], [:digit:]) not supported by Tier 2 regex emulation. Word boundary matching (-w) may not work correctly with complex regex patterns.

packages.name: jq
packages.porting_notes:
First 68k AmigaOS port of jq. A PPC-MorphOS port exists on Aminet (v1.8.1, Oct 2025) but is incompatible with classic 68k hardware.

Built with -O0 due to a bebbo-gcc 6.5.0 codegen bug (crash-patterns #16) that corrupts 16-byte struct returns at -O1/-O2.

Revision 2: Includes Oniguruma 6.9.9 regex engine (ASCII-only build, 227 KB). All regex builtins work: test, match, capture, scan, sub, gsub. Named capture groups supported. Single-entry regex compilation cache for performance. Memory leak on regex error path fixed.

Built without decNumber: uses standard IEEE 754 doubles for JSON number arithmetic. Uses -std=gnu99 (ADR-022) for jq's 60+ C99 for-init declarations.

128KB stack cookie. pthread TLS replaced with static singleton stubs (single-threaded AmigaOS). fdopen()+open() replaced with fopen()+stat() (crash-patterns #12). Large local buffer in jv_file.c made static (crash-patterns #10).

Reviewed by memory-checker (15 leak paths fixed across 3 rounds), perf-optimizer (output buffering, MurmurHash alignment, escape batching, regex cache applied), hardware-expert (stack and alignment verified for 68000-68060), and profiler (87% startup cost confirmed, linear scaling).

60/60 FS-UAE tests pass (50 original + 10 regex).
packages.known_limitations: ASCII-only regex: Unicode character properties (\p{Letter}) not supported. All other regex features work. No arbitrary-precision decimals: uses IEEE 754 doubles (1.1 + 2.2 != 3.3). GNU math extensions (j0, j1, yn, tgamma, cbrt, erf) return runtime errors. Slow startup (~1s on vamos for builtin compilation, ~7-12s on stock A1200) -- architectural to jq, not fixable at source level. Practical for config file processing on accelerated A1200 or better.

packages.readme:
Short:        Lua lightweight scripting language
Type:         dev/lang
Architecture: m68k-amigaos >= 3.0
Uploader:     duncan@bowring.us
Author:       Roberto Ierusalimschy, PUC-Rio (ported by Duncan Bowring)
Version:      5.4.7

lua - Lua 5.4.7 scripting language for AmigaOS

Original authors: Roberto Ierusalimschy, Luiz Henrique de Figueiredo,
Waldemar Celes (PUC-Rio). MIT License.

Ported by Duncan Bowring using amiport (AI-assisted porting toolkit).
Project: https://github.com/bdgscotland/amiport

First port of Lua 5.4 to any Amiga platform. Previous ports (5.2.1, 2012)
are 14 years old and two major versions behind.

Cross-compiled with m68k-amigaos-gcc for AmigaOS 3.x (68000+).
Tested on FS-UAE (65/65 tests passing). Standalone binary, no ixemul required.

New in Lua 5.4 (vs old Amiga port at 5.2):
  - Native integer type with bitwise operators (&, |, ~, <<, >>)
  - Integer floor-division operator (//)
  - Generational garbage collector
  - To-be-closed variables (<close>)
  - utf8 standard library
  - Warning system (warn())

Usage:
  lua                           Start interactive REPL
  lua script.lua                Run a Lua script
  lua -e "print('hello')"      Evaluate an expression
  lua -v                        Show version

Known limitations:
  - io.popen() not supported (returns Lua error)
  - No dynamic C module loading (pure Lua require() works)
  - 32-bit integers (range: -2147483648 to 2147483647)
  - os.execute() uses AmigaDOS Execute()

Stack: 256KB (set via __stack cookie)
Binary size: ~253KB

PORT.md documents every transformation applied.
Full source: https://github.com/bdgscotland/amiport/tree/main/ports/lua
packages.porting_notes: Only 5 of 61 source files needed changes. LUA_USE_C89 cascades integer/number type selection from long long to long/double. Custom lua_tmpnam routes os.tmpname() to T: (RAM disk) since libnix tmpnam() generates broken /tmp/ paths. The -fbaserel flag was tried and rejected — it corrupts A4-relative addressing for Lua's extensive global state (crash-patterns #13). LUA_32BITS (float instead of double) also rejected — generates Line F FPU traps on 68000 (crash-patterns #14). Two perf optimizations applied: localeconv() override (AmigaOS always uses C locale) and tolower() replaced with bitwise OR in pattern matching. Stack increased to 256KB for deep recursion and module loading. 65/65 FS-UAE tests passing on Workbench 3.1.
packages.known_limitations: io.popen() not supported (returns a Lua error gracefully). No dynamic C module loading. Integers are 32-bit (long) rather than 64-bit. os.execute() uses AmigaDOS Execute() with different return code semantics. isatty() always returns true.

mg - Micro Emacs-like text editor

A lightweight, public domain Emacs clone from the BSD family.
Provides core Emacs keybindings in a small footprint without
requiring Lisp or heavy dependencies.

Original author: Joachim Wiberg (troglobit/mg), OpenBSD
Ported by Duncan Bowring using amiport (AI-assisted porting toolkit).
Project: https://github.com/bdgscotland/amiport

Cross-compiled with m68k-amigaos-gcc for AmigaOS 3.x (68000+).
Tested in FS-UAE emulator with automated interactive tests.
Uses built-in ANSI terminal backend (no ncurses required).

Usage:
  mg [-hnR] [-f mode] [-u file] [+number] [file ...]

  mg WORK:document.txt       Open a file for editing
  mg -R WORK:readme.txt      Open in read-only mode
  mg +50 WORK:source.c       Open at line 50
  mg -n WORK:file.txt        Open without backup files

Key bindings (Emacs-style):
  C-x C-f   Find (open) file
  C-x C-s   Save file
  C-x C-c   Quit
  C-s       Search forward
  C-r       Search backward
  C-k       Kill (cut) line
  C-y       Yank (paste)
  C-x u     Undo
  C-g       Cancel current operation

Configuration:
  Place startup commands in S:.mg

Disabled features (no fork/exec on AmigaOS):
  - Shell commands (M-x shell-command)
  - Directory editor (dired)
  - Cscope/grep integration
  - Transparent .gz file opening
  - Suspend to shell (C-z)

Includes full source code (original POSIX and ported Amiga versions)
and PORT.md documenting every transformation applied.
packages.porting_notes: 43-file Category 3 (Console UI) port with built-in ANSI terminal backend (-DWITHOUT_CURSES), no ncurses dependency. Five subsystems disabled due to AmigaOS fork/exec absence: dired, compile/grep mode, cscope, M-x shell-command, and transparent .gz open. Console I/O uses Open("*") + SelectInput/SelectOutput for interactive console handle under ARexx ITEST harness. Two critical memory leaks fixed post-audit: interpreter.c getenv() result for M-x get-environment-variable, and ttykbd.c TERM env var leaked on startup. All 17 functional tests and 7 visual tests (ADR-024 SCRAPE pass) pass on FS-UAE A1200 with Kickstart 3.1.
packages.known_limitations: No shell integration (M-x shell-command, M-!, C-z suspend) -- AmigaOS has no fork(). No dired (directory editor) -- requires fork/exec for shell commands. No cscope/grep/compile mode -- requires popen(). No transparent .gz file opening -- requires popen("gunzip"). Terminal size fixed at startup (no SIGWINCH resize detection). Tilde expansion (~user) only works for current user via ENV:HOME. File permissions (chmod/chown) are no-ops.

tetris - Terminal tetris game from OpenBSD

The classic BSD Tetris game, running in your Amiga shell window.
This is the first terminal-mode tetris for AmigaOS -- all existing
Amiga tetris games use Intuition windows.

Original authors: Chris Torek and Darren F. Provine (UC Berkeley).
Ported by Duncan Bowring using amiport (AI-assisted porting toolkit).
Project: https://github.com/bdgscotland/amiport

Cross-compiled with m68k-amigaos-gcc for AmigaOS 3.x (68000+).
Tested on AmigaOS 3.1 via FS-UAE emulator.

Usage:
  tetris [-cps] [-k keys] [-l level]

Options:
  -c        Classic mode (different rotation, no reverse video)
  -k keys   Custom key bindings (6 chars: left rotate right drop pause quit)
  -l level  Starting level 1-9 (default: 2, higher = faster)
  -p        Show preview of next piece (score penalty)
  -s        Show high scores and exit

Default keys:
  j - move left      k - rotate     l - move right
  <space> - drop     p - pause      q - quit

High scores are saved to PROGDIR:tetris.scores

Requirements:
  AmigaOS 3.0+, 68000+, any console window

Known limitations:
  - Timer granularity ~20ms (vs nanosecond on Unix). Playable at all levels.
  - All scores recorded under "amiga" unless LOGNAME/USER env vars are set.
  - No job control (Ctrl-Z) -- AmigaOS has no job control.

Includes full source code (original POSIX and ported Amiga versions)
and PORT.md documenting every transformation applied.
packages.porting_notes:
The first terminal-mode tetris for AmigaOS. All existing Amiga tetris games are graphical Intuition applications -- this one runs in any shell window.

The game timing loop was completely redesigned: the Unix ppoll()+clock_gettime() approach was replaced with AmigaOS WaitForChar() from dos.library, which provides ~20ms granularity (one Amiga tick) without requiring any POSIX timer infrastructure. The game is playable at all 9 speed levels.

Display uses the amiport console-shim termcap API (tgetent/tgetstr/tgoto/tputs) -- no curses dependency. This port also improved the console-shim library itself: added classic termcap globals (PC, BC, UP), optimized tputs() from per-character callbacks to fputs(), and replaced snprintf() in tgoto() with direct digit emission for a 10-30x render path speedup.

Score file is stored in PROGDIR:tetris.scores (no hidden dot prefix, since AmigaOS does not use hidden files). The fdopen(open()) pattern in scores.c was replaced with fopen() per crash-patterns #12. 18 FS-UAE functional tests pass across all flags and interactive gameplay paths.
packages.known_limitations:
- All scores recorded under username "amiga" unless LOGNAME or USER environment variables are set
- No job control (Ctrl-Z) -- AmigaOS has no job control subsystem
- Board is fixed at 20 rows regardless of terminal window size (upstream design)
- Timer granularity is ~20ms (AmigaOS tick rate) vs nanosecond on Unix; playable at all 9 levels

packages.name: vim
packages.porting_notes:
Showcase port — first modern vim for 68k AmigaOS in 25 years (last working 68k port was vim 5.8, ~1998). Vim has native Amiga support via os_amiga.c maintained since its first public release on Fred Fish Disk 591 in 1992, making this a 34-year homecoming rather than a ground-up port.

Only 4 surgical changes to 2 upstream files: (1) __stack cookie (262144 bytes) for OS3/bebbo-gcc path — OS4 uses a 1MB stack cookie that exceeds OS3 RAM budgets; (2) pr_WindowPtr = -1 in mch_init() to suppress AmigaDOS volume requesters during startup path probing — vim queries dozens of config paths ($VIM/vimrc, $VIMRUNTIME/defaults.vim, home:.vimrc etc.) and a binary named 'vim' causes AmigaDOS to probe 'vim:' as a volume reference, triggering a blocking requester (crash-patterns #22, newly discovered during this port); (3) safe_Lock() wrapper for pre-init Lock() calls with save/restore of pr_WindowPtr; (4) gethostname() fallback returning 'amiga' (libnix has no gethostname). Two new files added: vim_stubs.c (8 no-op stubs for X11 input method and missing POSIX functions) and pathdef.c (Amiga-friendly compiled-in paths: S:vim, S:vim/runtime).

Build configuration: -Os -m68020 -std=gnu99 -noixemul -DFEAT_NORMAL. The -Os flag saves 465KB (2.7MB to 2.2MB) with negligible performance difference on 68020 (perf-optimizer finding). -m68020 is required — vim is too large for practical 68000 use at 93 source files / 385K lines. gnu99 is required — vim uses C99 pervasively (29K '//' comments, 130+ for-init declarations) and cannot be mechanically converted to C89. Built without ixemul.library dependency; runs standalone on any AmigaOS 3.0+ system.

FEAT_NORMAL gives: syntax highlighting, code folding, multiple windows and splits, Vimscript and Vim9 script, spell checking, diff mode, blowfish encryption, visual mode, unlimited undo, and file recovery via swap files in T:. FEAT_TERMINAL and FEAT_CSCOPE excluded (require PTY and fork/exec respectively — not available on AmigaOS).

Memory-checker review: PASS — one fix applied (NIL: handle leak in mch_check_win() error path, added Close(nilfh) before exit). Swap file cleanup via ml_close_all(TRUE) verified correct.

23/23 FS-UAE tests passing (21 TEST: + 2 ITEST:). Interactive tests limited to 2 due to 2.2MB binary exhausting 8MB FS-UAE fast RAM on a third ITEST launch. The :cq error exit code test uses non-interactive -c cq flag instead.
packages.known_limitations:
- No terminal job/channel support: FEAT_TERMINAL requires PTY, not available on AmigaOS
- No cscope integration: FEAT_CSCOPE requires fork/exec
- No clipboard or GUI: console-only, no Intuition integration
- No UTF-8: AmigaOS is single-byte; UTF-8 files display raw bytes
- Diff mode requires external diff command in PATH (ports/diff/ is available)
- Requires 68020 or higher CPU: vim is too large for practical 68000 use
- Runtime files not bundled: syntax highlighting requires separate vim runtime installation (copy to S:vim/runtime/ and set VIM=S:vim)
- vamos incompatible: 68020 instructions require FS-UAE for testing
- 4MB RAM recommended: binary is 2.2MB, loading large files needs headroom

vim 9.1 - Vi IMproved text editor

The first modern Vim for classic AmigaOS in 25 years.

The last working 68k Vim was version 5.8 from 1998. This port brings
Vim 9.1 (2024) to AmigaOS 3.x with FEAT_NORMAL: syntax highlighting,
code folding, split windows, Vimscript, Vim9 script, spell checking,
diff mode, blowfish encryption, and the full regex engine.

Vim 9.1 ships with built-in Amiga support (os_amiga.c) maintained since
the original Amiga release on Fred Fish Disk 591 in 1992. This port
required only 4 surgical changes to the upstream source -- a stack
cookie for OS3, a volume requester suppression, a gethostname fallback,
and libnix compatibility stubs. All changes are upstreamable.

Ported by Duncan Bowring using amiport (AI-assisted porting toolkit).
Project: https://github.com/bdgscotland/amiport

Cross-compiled with m68k-amigaos-gcc (bebbo) for AmigaOS 3.x (68020+).
Built with -Os -noixemul (standalone binary, no ixemul.library needed).
Tested on FS-UAE with Workbench 3.1 -- 23 automated tests passing.

Requirements:
  - 68020 or higher CPU (A1200, A4000, accelerated A500/A2000)
  - AmigaOS 3.0 or higher
  - 4MB RAM recommended (vim binary is 2.2MB)
  - No external libraries required (-noixemul build)

Installation:
  Copy vim to C: or any directory in your path.

  Optional: create S:vim/ for runtime files (syntax highlighting,
  color schemes, help files). Set the VIM environment variable:
    setenv VIM S:vim

  Without runtime files, vim works as a fully functional editor
  with all core features -- just no syntax coloring or help.

Usage:
  vim file.txt              Edit a file
  vim -R file.txt           Read-only mode
  vim -o file1 file2        Open files in split windows
  vim -c "set number" file  Open with line numbers enabled
  vim -u NONE file          Start without loading any config

  In vim:
    i          Enter insert mode (type text)
    ESC        Return to normal mode
    :w         Save file
    :q         Quit (or :q! to force quit without saving)
    :wq        Save and quit
    /pattern   Search forward
    dd         Delete current line
    u          Undo last change
    :split     Split window horizontally

Features enabled (FEAT_NORMAL):
  - Syntax highlighting (requires runtime files in $VIMRUNTIME)
  - Code folding
  - Multiple windows and splits
  - Vimscript and Vim9 script
  - Spell checking
  - Diff mode (requires external diff command)
  - Blowfish encryption (:X to encrypt a file)
  - Visual mode selection
  - Unlimited undo/redo
  - File recovery via swap files (stored in T:)

Known limitations:
  - No terminal job/channel support (needs PTY, not available on AmigaOS)
  - No cscope integration (needs fork/exec)
  - No clipboard/GUI integration (console-only)
  - No UTF-8 (AmigaOS is single-byte; UTF-8 files show raw bytes)
  - Diff mode requires a separate diff command in the PATH

Build info:
  Compiler: m68k-amigaos-gcc 6.5.0b (bebbo)
  Flags:    -Os -noixemul -m68020 -std=gnu99 -DFEAT_NORMAL

Original author: University of California, Berkeley

Project: https://github.com/bdgscotland/amiport

Cross-compiled with m68k-amigaos-gcc for AmigaOS 3.x (68000+).

Usage:
  wc [file ...]           Count lines, words, and bytes
  wc -l file              Count lines only
  wc -w file              Count words only
  wc -c file              Count bytes only
  wc -h file              Human-readable byte counts (e.g., 1.2K)
  wc file1 file2          Count multiple files with total

Notes:
  - The -m flag (multibyte characters) is accepted but equivalent
    to -c on AmigaOS (no multibyte locale support).
  - Exit codes: 0 (success), 10 (error) per AmigaOS convention.
  - Supports Amiga wildcard expansion in filenames.

Includes full source code (original POSIX and ported Amiga versions)
and PORT.md documenting every transformation applied.
packages.porting_notes: Easy single-file port with all Tier 1 issues; the main subtlety was that MB_CUR_MAX in bebbo-gcc expands to a runtime function call rather than a constant, so the multibyte character path had to be guarded with #ifdef __AMIGA__ to prevent it from being entered on AmigaOS where wchar/locale support is absent.
packages.known_limitations: -m flag (multibyte characters) is accepted but operates identically to -c (no multibyte locale on AmigaOS 3.x). -h uses a simplified fmt_scaled() implementation rather than OpenBSD libutil. fstat() on directories returns st_size=0.

yes — Repeatedly output a string (OpenBSD)

Original author: The Regents of the University of California
Ported by Duncan Bowring using amiport (AI-assisted porting toolkit).
Project: https://github.com/bdgscotland/amiport

Cross-compiled with m68k-amigaos-gcc for AmigaOS 3.x (68000+).
Tested in vamos emulator.

Usage:
  yes [string]

  With no arguments, outputs "y" repeatedly.
  With an argument, outputs that argument repeatedly.
  Press Ctrl-C to stop.

Examples:
  yes              ; outputs "y" forever until Ctrl-C
  yes n            ; outputs "n" forever until Ctrl-C
  yes | delete >NIL: RAM:tempfile  ; auto-confirm deletion

Includes full source code (original POSIX and ported Amiga versions)
and PORT.md documenting every transformation applied.
packages.porting_notes: Trivially small port (17 executable lines) but the infinite loop required adding amiport_check_break() for Ctrl-C support — without it, the user would have no way to stop the program since AmigaOS has no OS-level SIGINT delivery.
packages.known_limitations: Outputs only the first argument when multiple arguments are given, matching OpenBSD behavior (GNU yes concatenates all arguments).
